4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The space giant is rapidly pivoting into a global compute empire, and their latest massive contract proves that the war for Nvidia GB300 chips is moving to Memphis.
Something massive is happening in Memphis, Tennessee. While the world was focused on SpaceX’s historic IPO and rocket launches, Elon Musk’s company has been quietly building the most formidable third-party compute platform on the planet.
In a monumental shift for the artificial intelligence industry, SpaceX has signed a compute power agreement with open-source AI startup Reflection AI worth up to $6.3 billion through 2029.
Starting July 1, 2026, Reflection AI will pay $150 million per month to access Nvidia’s bleeding-edge GB300 chips housed at SpaceX’s Colossus 2 data center.
If you are an engineer, a founder, or a tech enthusiast, you need to pay attention. This is not just a real estate or cloud hosting deal; it is a fundamental rewiring of how frontier AI models will be trained, funded, and deployed.
Here is why this $6.3 billion contract is the most important AI news of the year, and what it means for the future of open-source development.
The Rise of Colossus: From Grok to a Compute Empire
To understand the magnitude of this deal, we have to look at the infrastructure. Colossus was originally launched in September 2024 at a former Electrolux site in Memphis, initially designed solely to train xAI’s language model, Grok. Built at breakneck speed, the original facility was powered by 100,000 Nvidia GPUs and went live in just 122 days.
But SpaceX realized they were sitting on a goldmine. Compute capacity is the single biggest bottleneck in the AI arms race. SpaceX pivoted, expanding their infrastructure and opening their doors to external AI companies.
The client list is staggering. SpaceX has already secured massive compute agreements with industry heavyweights like Anthropic (which rented all capacity at the Colossus 1 data center as of May 2026), Google, and Cursor. With the addition of Reflection AI, SpaceX’s committed revenues from outside clients now exceed $80 billion through 2029.
Who is Reflection AI?
You might be wondering how a startup barely two years old is signing multi-billion dollar compute contracts.
Reflection AI was founded in 2024 by Misha Laskin and Ioannis Antonoglou, both former researchers at Google DeepMind (where Antonoglou famously helped build AlphaGo).
Unlike closed-lab competitors such as OpenAI and Anthropic, Reflection is completely dedicated to “open weights” and open-source foundation models. Their mission is to build “American open intelligence” and ensure that “superintelligence” is accessible to anyone, mirroring the open ethos of Linux and the early internet.
This vision has attracted massive capital. After a $2 billion raise backed by Nvidia, Sequoia, and Lightspeed, Reflection AI is now raising funds at a staggering $25 billion valuation.
The Nvidia Circular Economy
There is a fascinating financial architecture behind this deal. Nvidia is on both sides of the table.

• Nvidia manufactures the top-of-the-line GB300 chips that power Colossus 2.

• Nvidia is also a major investor in Reflection AI, having put approximately $800 million into the startup.
Money flows from Nvidia to the startup, which then pays SpaceX to rent the hardware that Nvidia built. This circular flow of capital and silicon is becoming the defining financial model of the modern AI buildout.
What This Means for Developers (With Code)
For the developer community, Reflection’s massive compute upgrade means we are about to see incredibly powerful open-source models hit the market. If Reflection delivers on its promise of frontier-level open models, building complex, large-scale AI applications will no longer require passing sensitive data to closed APIs.
Imagine deploying a highly scalable, agentic application using an open-source Reflection model hosted on your own virtualized infrastructure. Here is a conceptual example of how a developer might interface with an open-weights model for high-throughput batch processing using Python and a standard ASGI framework like FastAPI:
Python
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel
import torch
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer# Conceptual initialization of a massive open-source model
# In reality, this would be sharded across multiple GPUs using something like vLLM
MODEL_NAME = "reflection-ai/reflection-frontier-instruct"print("Loading model weights (This requires serious VRAM)...")
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL_NAME)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
MODEL_NAME,
device_map="auto",
torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16
)app = FastAPI(title="Local Reflection AI Inference API")class PromptRequest(BaseModel):
prompt: str
max_tokens: int = 512
temperature: float = 0.7@app.post("/v1/completions")
async def generate_completion(request: PromptRequest):
try:
inputs = tokenizer(request.prompt, return_tensors="pt").to("cuda") # Generating output using the local hardware
outputs = model.generate(
**inputs,
max_new_tokens=request.max_tokens,
temperature=request.temperature,
do_sample=True
)
response_text = tokenizer.decode(outputs[0], skip_special_tokens=True)
return {"choices": [{"text": response_text}]}
except Exception as e:
raise HTTPException(status_code=500, detail=str(e))
# Run via: uvicorn main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000By securing the compute at Colossus 2, Reflection AI is guaranteeing they have the runway to build the world’s best open models at scale. When these models drop, architectures like the one above will become the standard for enterprise teams who want total control over their AI deployments.
The Bottom Line
SpaceX is no longer just a space company; it is the backbone of the AI revolution. The $6.3 billion deal with Reflection AI proves that the future of open-source artificial intelligence will be forged in massive data centers powered by tens of thousands of GPUs.
The contract includes a 90-day termination clause after the initial three months, giving both sides flexibility, but the sheer scale of the commitment signals that open-source AI is here to stay, and it is ready to compete with the biggest closed models on the planet.
